source_metadata.title_raw: Robin Paige Donoghue - A-19-079 - May 20, 2019 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Robin Paige Donoghue 
Town Attorney 
Town of Windsor 
555 Fifth Street, Suite 320 
Santa Rosa, CA 95401 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-079 
Dear Ms. Donoghue, 
May 20, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Town of Windsor Mayor 
Dominic Foppoli and Planning Commissioner Clayton Fritz regarding the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice 
under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under any other conflict of interest laws. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71 ), and any advice we provi~e assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case 
or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Mayor Foppoli or Planning Commissioner Fritz from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the Mill Creek Development Project, a residential development 
project for the construction of 360 condominium units on a 20-acre project site, given that the 
Project is located approximately 345 feet from commercial real property in which the Mayor holds 
a 30-percent ownership interest and the Commissioner holds a 10-percent ownership interest? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits both the Mayor and the Commissioner from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on both the Mayor's and the Commissioner's respective real property 
interests in the commercial real property located approximately 345 feet from the Project. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-079 
Page No. 2 
The Town of Windsor will soon consider decisions relating to a development application for 
the Mill Creek Development Project (the "Project"), a residential development project for the 
construction of 360 condominium units as well as a pool, spa and club room on a 20-acre project 
site (the "Project Site") which is currently vacant. The Project would also include over 500 parking 
spaces and various circulation improvements on the Project Site. In order for the Project to proceed, 
the Town would be required to approve a tentative subdivision map, site plan, design review, and 
conditional use permit for the Project. Centrally located within the Town, the Project Site is within 
walking distance of the Town's old downtown district, which includes numerous retail shops and 
restaurants, mixed-use residential development, and a transit center and commuter rail station site. 
The old downtown district also includes the 4.5-acre Town Greene, a centrally located park at 
which numerous community events and gatherings are held. 
You are the Windsor Town Attorney and the authorized representative of Windsor Mayor 
Dominic Foppoli and Planning Commissioner Clayton Fritz. The Mayor owns a 30-percent interest, 
and the Commissioner owns a 10-percent interest, in commercial real property located at 9000 
Windsor Road (the "Windsor Road Property"). There is a commercial building on the Windsor 
Road Property that is leased to a brewery (the "Brewery"), and the Brewery's operations at that 
location are limited to retail sales and tastings. The Mayor and the Commissioner each own five 
percent of the Brewery's intellectual property and brand, but would only receive compensation for 
owning those interests if the Brewery is sold at some point in the future. The Brew~ry leases the 
Windsor Road Property for $3,500 per month plus seven percent of the Brewery's monthly revenue. 
The property line of the Windsor Rqad Property is located approximately 345 feet from the nearest 
property line of the Project Site as the crow flies. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. With respect to 
decisions relating to the Project, Mayor Foppoli has the following interests: 
• 
A business interest in the Brewery due to the Mayor's five percent interest in its intellectual 
property and brand assuming that investment is worth $2,000 dr more. (See Section 87103(a).) 
• 
A business interest in the real property management business that leases the Windsor Road 
Property to the Brewery assuming the Mayor has an investment of $2,000 or more in that 
business. (See Section 87103(a).) 
• 
A real property interest in the Windsor Road Property in which the Mayor owns a 30 percent 
interest assuming that interest is worth $2,000 or more. (See Section 87103(b).) 


File No. A-19-079 
Page No. 3 
• 
A source of income interest in the Brewery if the Mayor has a IO-percent interest or greater in 
the real property management business (see Section 82030(a)), and if he aggregates $500 or 
more from the Brewery in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue (see Section 87103(c)). 
• 
A source of income interest in the real property management business that leases the Windsor 
Road Property to the Brewery if the Mayor aggregates $500 or more in income from the 
business in the l2 months prior to the decision at issue. (See Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in the Mayor's personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his personal finances. 
With respect to those decisions, Commissioner Fritz has the following interests: 
• 
A business interest in the Brewery due to the Commissioner's five percent interest in the 
Brewery's intellectual property and brand assuming that investment is worth $2,000 or more. 
(See Section 87103(a).) 
• 
A business interest in the real property management business that leases the Windsor Road 
Property to the Brewery assuming the Commissioner has an investment of $2,000 or more in 
that business. (See Section 87103(a).) 
• 
A real property interest in the Windsor Road Property in which the Commissioner owns a 10 
percent interest assuming that interest is worth $2,000 or more. (See Section 87103(b).) 
• 
A source of income interest in the Brewery if the Commissioner has a 10-percent interest or 
greater in the real property management business (see Section 82030(a)), and if he aggregates 
$500 or more from the Brewery in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue (see Section 
87103(c)). 
• 
A source of income interest in the real property management business that leases Windsor Road 
Property to the Brewery if the Commissioner aggregates $500 or more in income from the 
business in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue. (See Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in the Commissioner's personal finances and those of immediate family members. 
(See Section 87103.) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701 (b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a 
decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides 


File No. A-19-079 
Page No. 4 
that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
Neither the Mayor's nor the Commissioner's interests at issue are explicitly involved in 
decisions relating to the Project. 
Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable effect on an 
official's real property interest is material whenever the decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 
feet or less from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that 
the decision will not have any measurable impact on the official's property." 
The Windsor Road Property is located within approximately 345 feet of the Project Site. 
The Project Site is currently vacant, and the potential construction of 360 residential condominium 
units on the Project Site would increase the income producing potential of the Windsor Road 
Property and substantially alter the intensity of use, traffic levels, noise levels, and air quality of the 
area surrounding the Project Site, including the location of the Windsor Road Property. 
Furthermore, decisions relating to the Project may increase the market value of the Windsor Road 
Property because businesses may be willing to pay more to rent or purchase that property in order to 
take advantage of increased traffic from Project residents. Therefore, there is not clear and 
convincing evidence that decisions relating to the Project would not have a measurable impact on 
the Windsor Road Property. 
Accordingly, the Act prohibits both the Mayor and the Commissioner from taking part in 
decisions relating to the Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that they would have a material 
financial effect on the Mayor and the Commissioner's respective real property interests in the 
Windsor Road Property.2 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
Ge~~~/' 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 We do not analyze the effect of decisions relating to the Project on either the Mayor or the Commissioner's 
other interests at issue because we have already determined that those decisions will have a disqualifying effect on their 
respective real property interests in the Windsor Road Property.

interests in the commercial real property located approximately 345 feet from the Project.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Mayor Foppoli or Planning Commissioner Fritz from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to the Mill Creek Development Project, a residential development 
project for the construction of 360 condominium units on a 20-acre project site, given that the 
Project is located approximately 345 feet from commercial real property in which the Mayor holds 
a 30-percent ownership interest and the Commissioner holds a 10-percent ownership interest?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits both the Mayor and the Commissioner from taking part in decisions 
relating to the Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on both the Mayor's and the Commissioner's respective real property 
interests in the commercial real property located approximately 345 feet from the Project.
